name: Ahuitzotl Creature
description: The Ahuitzotl is a mythical creature from Aztec mythology, described as a dog-like or otter-like animal with smooth, dark fur, small, pointed ears, and a long, prehensile tail with a hand-like appendage at the end. It is said to inhabit the waterways and lakes of central Mexico, particularly those near the city of Tenochtitlan. The Ahuitzotl is believed to lure unsuspecting victims to their watery deaths by imitating the cries of a distressed infant. Once close enough, it uses its hand-like tail to drag them beneath the surface, drowning them. According to legend, the Ahuitzotl's victims are always found days later, their bodies intact but missing their eyes, teeth, and nails. These missing body parts were supposedly sent to Tlaloc, the rain god, as offerings. The creature is considered a harbinger of death and is greatly feared by those who live near the water. Its name, 'ahuitzotl,' translates to 'water dog' or 'thorny water thing' in Nahuatl, the Aztec language, reflecting both its aquatic nature and dangerous reputation. Some scholars suggest the Ahuitzotl may be based on real animals, such as the water opossum or the otter, embellished with mythical elements to serve as a cautionary tale about the dangers of the natural world.
subtitle: Water Dog of Aztec Legend
appearance: The Ahuitzotl is described as having a dog-like or otter-like body covered in smooth, dark fur. It has small, pointed ears and a long, prehensile tail ending in a hand-like appendage. Some accounts also mention it having webbed feet, allowing for greater agility in the water. Its face is said to be expressive, capable of mimicking human emotions, which aids in its deceptive tactics. The creature's size is generally depicted as being similar to that of a small dog or otter.
habitat: The Ahuitzotl is said to inhabit lakes, rivers, and other waterways of central Mexico, particularly those surrounding the ancient city of Tenochtitlan. It prefers deep waters and secluded areas where it can ambush its prey undetected.
behavior: The Ahuitzotl is a cunning and predatory creature. It uses its ability to mimic the cries of a distressed infant to lure victims to the water's edge. Once within reach, it uses its prehensile tail to drag them underwater, drowning them. It is said to be fiercely territorial and will attack anyone who ventures too close to its lair. The creature is also believed to be a messenger of Tlaloc, the rain god, and its actions are seen as a reflection of his will.

keyMyths.title: The Lure of the Waters
keyMyths.description: The Ahuitzotl lures people to their deaths by mimicking the cries of a baby. Once close enough, it uses its hand-like tail to pull them into the water, drowning them and taking their eyes, teeth, and nails as offerings to Tlaloc.
keyMyths.source: Various Aztec oral traditions and codices

sources.author: Bernardino de Sahagún
sources.work: General History of the Things of New Spain (Florentine Codex)
sources.description: A detailed account of Aztec culture and beliefs, including descriptions of mythical creatures like the Ahuitzotl.
